metadata.title: A packet spreads because its fast parts run ahead

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Salut, Liam — in for Bear. Why does that cause the packet to spread, and why does a narrower packet spread faster? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: Why does that cause the packet to spread, and why does a narrower packet spread faster??
props.segment: A packet spreads because its fast

beats.narration_text: A quantum particle can't hold its shape. The narrower you make it, the faster it smears.
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow QUANTUM MECHANICS, headline 'A quantum particle
can't hold its shape.
The narrower, the faster it smears.'

beats.narration_text: A localized electron is a bundle of momentum components. Each component travels at its own speed. Why does that cause the packet to spread, and why does a narrower packet spread faster?
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow THE QUESTION, headline 'Narrow packet =
wide momentum range =
faster spreading. Why?'

beats.narration_text: Start with a Gaussian wave packet at time zero. It is a localized bump in position space. Its Fourier transform — the momentum content — is also a Gaussian, centered on the central momentum p sub zero. The width in momentum is proportional to h-bar divided by the width in position.
beats.visual: Left: Gaussian in position space (TEAL), labeled delta x. Right: Gaussian in momentum space (CRIMSON), labeled delta p. Arrow showing delta x * delta p = h-bar / 2.

beats.narration_text: Each momentum component p travels at speed p over m. Low-momentum components — shown in teal — travel slowly. High-momentum components — shown in crimson — travel fast. The packet is a superposition of all these components at once.
beats.visual: Multiple sine-wave-like components with different k values drawn across a horizontal axis. Left group (TEAL) slower arrow, right group (CRIMSON) faster arrow showing different speeds.

beats.narration_text: Watch the packet at time zero — a tight Gaussian. Now at time t equals 1. The fast components have run ahead; the slow ones lag behind. The packet has flattened and widened. This is purely kinematic: different speeds, same starting position, separation is inevitable.
beats.visual: Two side-by-side panels: t=0 (tight GOLD Gaussian) and t=1 (flat, wider Gaussian). Arrows indicating fast (CRIMSON right) and slow (TEAL left) components separating.

beats.narration_text: The spreading formula is exact: delta x of t equals delta x at zero, times the square root of one plus the quantity t h-bar over m delta x zero squared, all squared. For short times the spread grows linearly. For large times, it grows as t divided by m delta x zero — the packet is just the fast components running away.
beats.visual: Plot of delta x(t) vs t. Curve starts at delta x_0, then grows. Label the formula delta x(t) = delta x_0 * sqrt(1 + (t*hbar/m*delta_x0^2)^2). TEAL curve, SLATE axes.

beats.narration_text: The narrower the initial packet, the faster it spreads. A packet squeezed to one nanometer spreads much faster than one squeezed to one micron. Narrow position forces wide momentum, and wide momentum means large spread in speeds, which means rapid broadening.
beats.visual: Two curves delta x(t): narrow initial packet (CRIMSON, spreads fast) vs wide initial packet (TEAL, spreads slowly). Both on same time axis. Label delta x_0_narrow < delta x_0_wide.

beats.narration_text: This is not a mystery. It is kinematics plus Fourier. The uncertainty principle does not cause the spreading — it follows from the same mathematics. A localized anything — a sound pulse, a water ripple — spreads for the same reason if its components move at different speeds.
beats.visual: Two panels side by side: left QUANTUM (TEAL electron packet) and right CLASSICAL (SLATE water ripple). Both spreading. Label 'kinematics + Fourier' below both.

beats.narration_text: Numbers. An electron localized to delta x equals 1 nanometer. Its momentum spread is delta p equals h-bar over delta x, roughly 0.1 electron volt divided by the speed of light. The spreading timescale is m times delta x squared over h-bar, about 8.6 femtoseconds. Within 10 femtoseconds, the packet has doubled in size.
beats.visual: Numeric table: delta x_0 = 1 nm; delta p = hbar/delta x_0 ~ 0.1 eV/c; t_spread = m*delta_x_0^2/hbar ~ 8.6 fs. TEAL row headers, CRIMSON values. Emphasize the femtosecond timescale.

beats.narration_text: Narrow packet: wide momentum range. Wide momentum range: components separate. Components separate: packet spreads.
beats.visual:
CARD: eyebrow QUANTUM MECHANICS, headline 'Narrow in x = wide in p.
Wide in p = components separate.
Components separate = spreading.'

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Here's what the body just demonstrated. The narrower the initial packet, the faster it spreads. A packet squeezed to one nanometer spreads much faster than one squeezed to one micron. Narrow position forces wide momentum, and wide momentum means large spread in speeds, which means rapid broadening. This is not a mystery. It is kinematics plus Fourier. The uncertainty principle does not cause the spreading — it follows from the same mathematics. A localized anything — a sound pulse, a water ripple — spreads for the same reason if its components move at different speeds.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [A packet spreads because its fast parts run ahead] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
